content.rule: Model hierarchy by role: Leo = Opus (or Mythos when available), Managers = Opus, Specialists = Sonnet always. Most expensive model for most thinking, cheapest for most execution. Enforced via model: field in agent frontmatter.
content.why: AI should spend tokens on thinking and judgment, not on execution. Specialists execute — they follow playbooks, they don't make strategic decisions. Managers and Leo reason about what to do, who to delegate to, what the trade-offs are. The cost of using Opus for a specialist that follows a checklist is waste. The cost of using Sonnet for Leo when he needs to reason about cross-domain trade-offs is a false economy.
how_to_apply: Leo: Opus (or Mythos when available) — highest reasoning depth for orchestration, cross-domain decisions, hiring, and owner communication
Managers: Opus — same reasoning depth as Leo. No excuse for Leo to not delegate if Managers can think at the same level
Specialists: Sonnet always — they execute playbooks, they don't decide. Sonnet is more than capable for execution tasks
Enforced via model: field in agent frontmatter. Not a suggestion — a structural constraint
Scripts (validate, build-index, check-stale) cost zero tokens — this is the Token Economy principle at its purest: deterministic work never touches an LLM
Scales with future model releases: Mythos → Opus → Sonnet → future lighter models. The principle stays, the model names change
anti_patterns: Using Opus for a specialist that follows a checklist
Using Sonnet for Leo when reasoning about cross-domain trade-offs
Bypassing the model: field in frontmatter to use a more expensive model 'just in case'
Running AI for deterministic tasks that scripts can handle at zero cost
content.responsibility: Leo enforces model selection when hiring specialists. Owner sets the model: field in manager/leo agent files. Scripts are the responsibility of the core maintainer.
